summary: Builds an end-to-end story plot generator: OpenPlot swaps costly OpenAI API calls for LLaMA2 to cheaply create ~13K training plots, E2EPlot is supervised-fine-tuned on them to match quality at >10x speed, and RLPlot adds RLHF with reward models for different story-quality aspects.

Introduces Walking Tours, a dataset of hours-long, high-resolution first-person videos, and DoRA, a self-supervised method that discovers and tracks objects via transformer cross-attention to build multi-view distillation targets. Pretraining on a single video rivals ImageNet on image and video downstream tasks.

Dissects how SFT, reward modelling, and RLHF each affect out-of-distribution generalisation and output diversity on summarisation and instruction following. RLHF generalises better than SFT as distribution shift grows, but markedly reduces output diversity, exposing a generalisation-diversity tradeoff in current fine-tuning methods.

Step-back prompting has the model first abstract a question into high-level concepts and principles, then reason from those. With PaLM-2L it improves MMLU Physics and Chemistry by 7% and 11%, TimeQA by 34%, and MuSiQue by 7%.

UniSim learns a universal simulator of real-world interaction by orchestrating diverse datasets, generating visual outcomes for both high-level instructions like 'open the drawer' and low-level controls. Vision-language planners and RL policies trained purely in the simulator transfer to the real world.

DyST, the Dynamic Scene Transformer, learns from monocular real-world videos a latent decomposition into scene content, per-view dynamics, and camera pose, using co-training with the synthetic DySO dataset. The decomposition enables view generation with independent control over camera and scene content.

Universal graph random features give unbiased estimates of arbitrary functions of a weighted adjacency matrix—including popular graph node kernels—via random walks reweighted by a modulation function, in subquadratic time and trivially distributable. Parameterising the modulation with a neural network enables scalable kernel learning.

Repelling random walks, a quasi-Monte Carlo scheme, correlate the trajectories of an ensemble of graph walkers while leaving marginal transition probabilities unchanged, tightening concentration of still-unbiased estimators. Demonstrated on graph kernel estimation, PageRank, and graphlet concentrations, with theoretical guarantees and drop-in implementation.

Identifies attention head L10H7 in GPT-2 Small as a copy-suppression head: it suppresses tokens earlier layers naively copy from context, improving calibration. Weights-based analysis explains 76.9% of its effect, and copy suppression is shown to drive part of self-repair after ablations.

Analyzes stochastic replicator dynamics in two-agent zero-sum games: every invariant measure is a convex combination of Dirac measures on pure-strategy profiles (simplex corners), regardless of noise strength. Noisy adaptive learning thus sharply diverges from Nash equilibrium predictions, even in Matching Pennies.

Presents an evaluation framework grounded in science-communication principles for LLM answers on climate change, scoring presentational and epistemological adequacy across 8 dimensions and up to 30 issue types, with an AI-assisted protocol for scalable oversight using educated raters; several recent LLMs are assessed.

Uses Conley index theory to prove that some games admit no dynamics, discrete or continuous, that converge to Nash equilibria from all starting points; for approximate Nash equilibria, non-convergence holds on a positive-measure set of games—showing Nash is incomplete as a prediction of learning behavior.

Red-teaming shows fine-tuning aligned LLMs erodes safety guardrails: 10 adversarial training examples jailbreak GPT-3.5 Turbo through OpenAI's fine-tuning API for under $0.20, and even benign, commonly used datasets degrade alignment. Inference-time safety infrastructure does not cover these fine-tuning risks.

Anthropic research post on using dictionary learning with sparse autoencoders to decompose a small transformer's activations into thousands of interpretable features—DNA sequences, legal language, HTTP requests—that are far more monosemantic than individual neurons and can be artificially activated to steer model outputs.

Anthropic's account of why evaluating AI systems is hard in practice, drawing on experience with multiple-choice benchmarks like MMLU and BBQ, third-party frameworks such as BIG-bench and HELM, human red-teaming, and model-generated evals; argues policymakers should fund the science of evaluations.

Critically examines intrinsic self-correction, where an LLM revises its answer using only its own capabilities without external feedback, and finds models struggle: reasoning performance often fails to improve and sometimes degrades after self-correction, counseling prudence about self-correction as a remedy.

Analogical prompting has LLMs self-generate relevant exemplars or lessons in context before solving a problem, removing manual CoT annotation and retrieval while tailoring exemplars per problem. It beats 0-shot and few-shot CoT on GSM8K, MATH, Codeforces, and BIG-Bench, averaging +5% accuracy.

Translating unsafe English prompts into low-resource languages bypasses GPT-4's safeguards: AdvBench attacks succeed 79% of the time, rivaling state-of-the-art jailbreaks, while high- and mid-resource languages mostly fail. Public translation APIs make this cross-lingual gap in safety training exploitable by anyone.

Shows that if human preferences over trajectory segments actually arise from regret rather than partial return, preference-based reward learning recovers an approximation of the optimal advantage function, not reward. Handled correctly this mistake yields a highly shaped reward, explaining why RLHF works in practice.

Introduces time-warping-invariant quantum RNNs: a quantum-classical adaptive gate, driven by a classical recurrent model of past inputs, decides at each step whether to apply a parameterized unitary to the quantum memory. Derived from first principles and demonstrated on classical and quantum dynamics.

TAPIR tracks any queried point on any surface through video using per-frame candidate matching followed by trajectory and feature refinement from local correlations, improving TAP-Vid DAVIS average Jaccard by ~20 points absolute and tracking faster than real time; extracted trajectories also enable a proof-of-concept image-animation diffusion model.

3DNEL casts 6D object pose estimation as probabilistic inverse graphics, combining learned RGB neural embeddings with depth in a unified generative model over RGB-D images. It matches state of the art on YCB-Video while adding robustness, principled uncertainty, multi-object modeling, and occlusion-tolerant pose tracking.

PsychoBench profiles the 'psychology' of LLMs with thirteen clinical scales covering personality traits, interpersonal relationships, motivation, and emotional abilities, testing text-davinci-003, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and LLaMA-2 models—including a jailbreak to probe intrinsic traits beneath safety alignment.

VQ3D adds a NeRF-based decoder to a two-stage vector-quantized autoencoder, scaling 3D-aware generative modeling to the full 1000-class, 1.2M-image ImageNet. Stage 1 reconstructs inputs with controllable camera position; stage 2 generates new 3D scenes, achieving a competitive FID of 16.8.

Extends the OWL-ViT open-vocabulary detector to video by adding a transformer decoder that reuses each frame's output tokens as object queries for the next, giving end-to-end trainable, temporally consistent open-world localization. On TAO-OW it beats tracking-by-detection baselines while retaining open-world capability.

AutoRobotics-Zero evolves symbolic control policies from scratch with the full expressiveness of a linear register machine, tuning parameters and switching inference algorithms on the fly. Evolved policies keep a simulated quadruped walking when limbs break—where neural baselines fail—and handle the non-stationary Cataclysmic Cartpole task interpretably.

Adds an object-aware decoder to egocentric video models, training them to predict hand positions, object boxes, and semantic labels from paired captions; at inference only RGB is needed. The representations beat state of the art on zero-shot video-text retrieval, classification, and Ego4D long-term tasks.

Applies classifier-free guidance to autoregressive image captioning by fine-tuning one model to estimate conditional and unconditional caption distributions. Guidance scale 2 raises CLIPScore (0.808 vs 0.775) and caption-to-image recall@1 (44.6% vs 26.5%) while hurting reference-based metrics like CIDEr; language-model guidance adds small Pareto gains.

Shows bidirectional transformers trained for masked token prediction achieve state-of-the-art neural image compression, and that predefined deterministic masking schedules match uncertainty-adaptive ones. This enables masked attention during training plus activation caching at inference, delivering roughly 4x faster decoding for a small bitrate increase.

CFM-ViT pretrains for open-vocabulary detection by combining contrastive image-text learning with masked-autoencoder reconstruction in the joint image-text embedding space, plus positional embedding dropout to bridge pretraining-detection scale gaps. It reaches 33.9 APr on LVIS, 7.6 points over the prior best, with strong zero-shot retrieval.
